title: Root Bridge
desc: A visitor route over living roots needs its lines and stones checked before the day's traffic starts.

steps.text:
A broad hollow just off the main path is crossed by a bridge that no mason built. Four great roots rise from opposite banks, twist together over the gap, and flatten themselves just enough for careful feet. It is beautiful in the way forests can be beautiful when they have decided to permit passage, which means it is also the kind of thing sensible people inspect every morning.

Last night's mist left the bridge slick, and an afternoon lecture group is expected from Kiru City. A path hand is already uncoiling a replacement line. "The bridge is sound," she says, touching the nearest root with obvious respect. "But sound is not the same as safe."

The work can begin at the bridge itself, where the outer hand line needs to be retensioned, or at the approach, where three stepping stones have lifted unevenly in the moss and need to be set back true.

options.label: Retension the hand line along the outer arc
Reset the stepping stones at the mossy approach

steps.text:
The hand line is threaded through carved pegs set into the living wood, each one polished smooth by weather and palms. You work your way across the bridge slowly, drawing the rope taut a section at a time while the path hand watches the sag disappear.

Under your boots the roots are warm and faintly textured, less like timber than like something sleeping beneath bark. Sunlight reaches through the canopy in narrow bands and turns the drop below bright green. When the final knot is drawn tight, the bridge feels transformed. Not rigid, not deadened, simply readable. A body crossing it will know where support is without having to ask.

The path hand gives the line an approving pull. "Good. Now the bridge can be admired without becoming the whole day." In the Forest of Life, that counts as practical success.

options.label: Test the line once more and step aside

steps.text:
The approach stones are half hidden under silver moss, which is lovely until a boot lands on the wrong edge. You clear the growth away with a flat wooden blade, lever each stone up just enough to see where the gravel bed has shifted, and settle it back into place with careful pressure.

The roots nearest the bank seem to notice the work. Fine tendrils brush the stone rims and withdraw again as if testing whether you know what you are doing. By the second reset the path hand has stopped giving instructions. She just watches the line of the walkway return, one level surface at a time.

When the last stone sits true, the bridge entrance looks inevitable, the way well-kept paths often do. Visitors arriving later will never know how easy it would have been to twist an ankle here. They will simply walk out across living wood and remember the beauty more than the labor that protected it.

options.label: Brush off your hands and leave the approach sound
